item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked this. The concept was not groundbreaking, but the integrated story of Matthews willingly trading the memories of her late husband for CotBG intel did it for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Read it all. Good concept, could be taken further though. I'd go for a testing log.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty good but one thing I do like is how obvious the Church of the Broken God is redacted I would recommend either put redactions through the spaces or just remove the redactions entirely (which is what I would do) after all the Foundation isn’t shy about mentioning the Church in there reports

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think Dr. Marie █. Matthews would appreciate having her exact age redacted permanently locked at 29 according to the official record.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There are just too many problems for me to not downvote it right now. But the concept is too good to not be improved, either. You had a very solid idea, just need to improve the execution.

1- Take out the bit about GoIs in the Containment.
– hide block

1- Take out the bit about GoIs in the Containment. It is too hamfisted. From a in-universe standpoint this skip doesn't really make any difference on the matter: any sympathy for a GoI should be taken seriously regardless of any particular anomaly nearby.

2- Take a little more time on Description, well, describing it.
– hide block

2- Take a little more time on Description, well, describing it. You go very short on physical description and rush yourself into presenting the anomalous effect right on the first paragraph. At the same time it ends up lacking a little more suspense and the object becomes a little harder to imagine. Yes, I know we have a picture, but the reader should have a feeling of how it is to be near the thing.

3- Take such opportunity to distinguish between plates at each side.
– hide block

3- Take such opportunity to distinguish between plates at each side. Not only "right-side-left-side" is hard to follow, but it actually makes no sense, since the scale on the picture is round.

4- Take a look at other skips to get a hold of how to describe the anomaly.
– hide block

4- Take a look at other skips to get a hold of how to describe the anomaly. You rush things all around, and end up explaining things in a confusing order. You don't need to make it more a complex text, just reword it to be clearer.

5- Take the "Recovery" section to after the first log of tests and change its title to Incident something.
– hide block

5- Take the "Recovery" section to after the first log of tests and change its title to Incident something. This is quite a good idea you had, here, but again the execution lacks patience. Naming it "Recovery" makes it appear as being how the Foundation got the object, what it isn't. Then add story to it. We had no chance to know anything about the characters before, so we had no reason to comprehend or care. We have little context. It is too short a description to deliver the emotional impact it is meant to.

6- Take your time to treat the first batch of tests with more care.
– hide block

6- Take your time to treat the first batch of tests with more care. On most of them your goal is clear, but the test isn't actually careful enough to render that data. You know what the results will be and why, but the researchers shouldn't. If done right, you probably can get rid of the Analysis section on each test.

7- Take out the Analysis section of the last test.
– hide block

7- Take out the Analysis section of the last test. This is the "punch" of the article, but in this case it doesn't need to be said aloud. At this point, most readers should be able to reach this conclusion by themselves. If you really want to explain yourself, do this through a last comment from a Site Director or something like that. As it is now, you're telling the joke and then explaining it before we have time to laugh.

8- Take out some black-boxes and redaction from the article.
– hide block

8- Take out some black-boxes and redaction from the article. So many black-box in a row is awkward. You numbered the Task Force, you can number the Area. The sector could have a short description instead of number. Keep the number of breaches blackened. Give the MTF a nickname, if you need just borrow a MTF from the Background section of the wiki. If you can, identify the GoI you used here. If you can't, avoid referencing it by name four short paragraphs in a row.


All this is just me, of course. Take it with a grain of salt, but it should help with what I think was your intent here. The concept is fairly strong, and the last test was very good. But you need a better build up to reach it. And either redact her entire age, or put it, it makes no sense to redact how far she is in her twenties.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks so much. It looks much better too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, cropped it a bit and shrunk it down. Image displayed is now a tidy 25.4 KB, which should load pretty much instantaneously on mobile.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Please. I would appreciate it very much.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Author, your image is 1.38 MB, way larger than it needs to be and possibly an active reader deterrent for people reading on mobile devices with limited data plans. Would you like assistance with resizing it?

item.title: Updates
item.content:encoded: I decided to make a few changes. I hope that it is easier to understand now that there are a dedicated gain and loss sides. I also removed the interview. I will try to figure out a way to simplify the parameters section. Thanks for your input.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I definitely end in an upvote, but this one is a little bit hard to follow. I know it should be easy to visualize, but it's hard to remember what they're giving up and what they are receiving. I feel like one side of the balance should be dedicated to what is being given up, and one be dedicated to what is being received. It would make things easier. The explanation for how it chooses what memories to exchange was also really difficult to follow? I now get that weight affects "importance", and so does sentimental value, but the former I only got once I reached the testing logs. Also, the interview with the D-Class seems irrelevant. Other than that, I mean, I did like this. I just think it is confusing in a bad way — a "huh?" way instead of a "what??" way.

item.title: Image Credit and Shout out to those that helped me.
item.content:encoded:
This image is borrowed from:
Original Source: burst.shopify.com
Creator: Matthew Henry

https://burst.shopify.com/photos/scales-of-justice

Thanks to everyone who helped me with this article: Baronjoe, Uncle Nicolini, Dr_Kai, Zyn, Rounderhouse, Gabriel Jade, taylor_itkin, CyrusFiredawn, Tutulord, and everyone else in IRC chat. I greatly appreciate your help and will make sure to use what I have learned in future content I create.

Some of the characters from this can be found in http://www.scp-wiki.net/a-night-to-remember
